unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 8! In this unit you use English to talk about maths in real life. You count and name big numbers, all the way up to one million. You learn the words addition, subtraction, multiplication and division. You measure the size, height, weight and distance of things around you, using words like meter and centimeter. You also hunt for straight lines and repeating patterns. Maths is everywhere: in the market, in the kitchen and on the way to school. By the end you will solve a number puzzle, just like Amal and Sami do in this unit's story.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary section. Learn a few maths words each day.
Work through the six grammar lessons, which cover all eight patterns from the source.
Read the story “The Mystery of the Million Shells” and answer the comprehension questions.
Work through the four skills: Reading and listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the quiz and the self-assessment to see what you have learned.

visual.alt: Cartoon learners measuring seashells with rulers and writing the numbers in a book

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A word or symbol that shows how many things there are.
practiceSentences: Amal wrote the number of shells at the top of her page.
Each locker has a different number on its door.
Which number is missing from this counting song?
The teacher said, "Double the number and add one."
I counted every number from one to one hundred.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: n - u - m - b - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Stay with your helper, tell the tutor the number of windows in your room, and ask it to check your sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A very big number written 1,000,000, or one thousand thousands.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: There are more than a million stars in the sky.
practiceSentences: Sami tried to picture a million grains of sand on the shore.
A million seconds is more than eleven days.
How many zeros does one million have?
Nora said, "I wish I had a million books."
I imagine a million ants marching in a line.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - i - l - l - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask your helper to listen while the tutor reads big numbers aloud, and say which one is closest to a million.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Something that is true and can be checked or proven.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: It is a fact that a week has seven days.
practiceSentences: Teacher Yasmin wrote one surprising fact about numbers on the board.
It is a fact that spiders have eight legs.
Can you share one fact about the moon?
Adam said, "A fact can be checked and proven."
My favourite fact is that octopuses have three hearts.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper listening, say one maths fact and one opinion, then ask the tutor which one can be proven.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A design or sequence of things that repeats in the same order.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We saw a colour pattern: red, blue, red, blue.
practiceSentences: Nora spotted a repeating pattern in the classroom floor tiles.
The tiles make a pattern of stars and squares.
What comes next in this shape pattern?
Sami said, "I made a pattern with red and gold beads."
I found a number pattern counting by fives.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - a - t - t - e - r - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Clap a short pattern for your helper, then ask the tutor to describe the rule you used.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Going the same way without bending or curving, like a ruler edge.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Draw a straight line across the page.
practiceSentences: Amal drew a straight line from one corner to the other.
Use a ruler to keep your lines straight.
Is the road to the market straight or curved?
The teacher said, "Stand in a straight line, please."
I folded the paper along a straight edge.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - t - r - a - i - g - h - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Point out one straight edge and one curved edge to your helper, then ask the tutor to check your two sentences.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Putting numbers together to find how many you have altogether.
practiceSentences: Our team used addition to find the total number of shells.
Addition joins two numbers to make a total.
Can you solve this addition problem in your head?
Maya said, "Addition is easy with counting beads."
I practised addition with bottle tops at home.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - d - d - i - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor for three addition questions, answer them out loud, and let your helper mark them with you.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Taking one number away from another to see what is left.
practiceSentences: Sami checked his subtraction by counting the shells again.
Subtraction tells us how many are left over.
Is subtraction the opposite of addition?
Theo said, "Subtraction shows how many sweets remain."
I solved ten subtraction sums without any mistakes.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - u - b - t - r - a - c - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Tell the tutor a subtraction story about sharing fruit, and ask your helper whether the answer sounds right.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Adding the same number many times to find a total quickly.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We use multiplication to find how many apples are in 4 boxes.
practiceSentences: Multiplication turned ten bowls of shells into one quick answer.
Multiplication is a fast way of adding equal groups.
Which multiplication table are you learning now?
Leo said, "Multiplication helps me count eggs in trays."
I made a chart to learn my multiplication tables.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - u - l - t - i - p - l - i - c - a - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Say your five times table to your helper, then ask the tutor to explain why multiplication is faster than adding.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Splitting a number into equal parts so everyone gets the same.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Division helps us share dates fairly.
practiceSentences: Amal used division to give each team an equal share.
Division shares things equally between friends.
Can division split twelve dates among four children?
Sami said, "Division is sharing done with numbers."
I used division to hand out the art paper fairly.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - i - v - i - s - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Share ten counters between two people with your helper, then ask the tutor to name the division sum you made.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To find out the size, length or amount of something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We will measure how tall the table is.
practiceSentences: Sami will measure the longest shell with a clean ruler.
We measure the bean plants every Monday morning.
Can you measure the desk with this ruler?
The teacher said, "Measure twice before you cut."
I measure my height against the kitchen door.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - e - a - s - u - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Measure one book with your helper, then ask the tutor whether centimeters or meters suit that job better.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: How big or how small something is when you look at it.
practiceSentences: Amal compared the size of two shells before she wrote.
The shoes come in every size at the market.
What size is your reading book, big or small?
Adam said, "These boxes are the same size."
I sorted the buttons by size and colour.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Hold up two objects for your helper, say which size is bigger, and ask the tutor to check your comparison.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A unit of length equal to one hundred centimeters.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The table is two meters long.
practiceSentences: Our classroom door is almost one meter wide.
One meter is one hundred centimeters long.
Is the doorway more than a meter wide?
Maya said, "My jump measured one whole meter!"
I cut a meter of string for the kite.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Step out one meter on the floor with your helper, then ask the tutor how many centimeters you crossed.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: How tall a person or thing is from bottom to top.
practiceSentences: Teacher Yasmin recorded the height of every shell in centimeters.
The nurse checked each child's height and wrote it down.
What is the height of the classroom door?
Theo said, "My height went up two centimeters!"
I marked my height on the wall chart.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - e - i - g - h - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask your helper to measure your height, then tell the tutor the number and the unit you used.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: How heavy something is when you put it on the scales.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The weight of the watermelon is 3 kilos.
practiceSentences: The weight of a wet shell surprised the whole team.
The scales show the weight of the flour.
Can you guess the weight of this pumpkin?
Nora said, "The weight of my bag surprised me."
I compared the weight of a feather and a stone.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: w - e - i - g - h - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Lift two shopping items with your helper, guess each weight in grams, and ask the tutor to compare your guesses.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: How far it is from one place to another place.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The distance from school to my house is short.
practiceSentences: Nora paced the distance from the table to the window.
The map shows the distance between the two towns.
What distance can you run without stopping?
Adam said, "The distance to the market is short."
I measured the distance across the playground in steps.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - i - s - t - a - n - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Count your steps to the door with your helper watching, then ask the tutor to turn the steps into meters.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Read and say big numbers, up to one million.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: The Speaking 4 recording of the number list from ten to one million, plus correct answers to comprehension questions 8 and 9.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use the maths words addition, subtraction, multiplication and division.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: All four operations matched correctly in Activities 1 and used in the Writing 2 maths report.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Measure the size, height, weight and distance of things around you.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: A height, a weight and a distance, each with its unit, recorded in Speaking 1 and listed in the Writing 4 fact file.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Talk about straight lines and repeating patterns.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: A pattern rule explained aloud in Speaking 3 and the repeating part named in the Writing 3 paragraph.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Give clear instructions using imperatives, such as “Measure the height.”
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Four Writing 1 steps that each begin with a verb, and the imperative identified in quiz question 5.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Say what you own with have got, and what you can do with can and can't.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Correct has got and have got choices in Grammar Practice 2, plus the market lines recorded in Speaking 2.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Talk about the future with will, and explain reasons using because.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: A future plan and a because reason given in Speaking 5, and one revised because sentence in Writing 5.

readings.title: Maths Is Everywhere
readings.setting: A coastal primary school, a home and a market
readings.passageScript: Numbers are all around us. At the market, Amal uses addition to add up the prices. When they share the dates with four cousins, they use division. Amal measures the size of the flour bag and checks its weight. Walking home, she counts her steps to find the distance to their door. Maths helps us every day!

readings.title: A Pattern Poem
readings.setting: A coastal primary school, a home and a market
readings.passageScript:
One, two, buckle my shoe,
Three, four, count some more,
Five, six, shapes and sticks,
Straight and round, patterns found!

readings.title: The Measuring Challenge
readings.setting: A coastal primary school, a home and a market
readings.passageScript: “Good morning, class! Today we will measure three things. First, measure the height of your desk. Next, measure the distance from the door to the window. Then, find the weight of your school bag. Write each number in your book. You can use a meter stick. Ready? Begin!”

readings.title: Numbers Big and Small
readings.setting: A coastal primary school, a home and a market
readings.passageScript: “Ten. One hundred. One thousand. Ten thousand. One hundred thousand. One million! A million is a very big number — it is one thousand thousands.”

readings.title: The Mystery of the Million Shells
readings.setting: A coastal primary school, a home and a market
readings.passageScript:
It was a hot afternoon in Cape Coast. The sea breeze blew through the windows of Class 3B.
“Today,” said Teacher Yasmin, “we have a challenge!” The board read: “Can you find the right pattern to solve the number puzzle?” The class clapped.
Sami looked at the table. There were seashells, string, rulers and bowls. “Each team will measure the size, weight and distance between the shells,” said Teacher Yasmin. “Use your eyes, your hands and your maths.”
“What is the first step?” asked Amal.
“Measure the height of your tallest shell,” said the teacher.
Sami's team got to work. “This is a straight shell,” said Amal. “That one is curved.”
“This shell is 2 meters from the bowl,” said Sami. “Let's write that down,” said Amal. The teams wrote their numbers in their books:
Height: 8 cm. Distance: 2 meters. Weight: 150 grams.
“Let's do some addition and subtraction,” said Sami. Amal smiled. “And don't forget multiplication and division!”
Suddenly, Amal pointed at the board. “There's a clue!” she shouted. It read: “There are more than 500 shells. Fewer than a million. Use a pattern. Count carefully.”
Sami counted the shells in one bowl. “There are 25 in this one. And there are 10 bowls.”
“25 times 10 is …?” Amal asked. “Multiplication!” said Sami. “That's 250.”
“And what if we add the shells on the table?” Amal asked. Sami replied, “Then it is over 300. Maybe 400.” “Still far from a million,” Amal laughed.
“Can you measure this shell?” Sami asked. “I can't,” Amal said. “It is too small, and the line isn't straight.” “Use the other ruler,” said Sami. “It has better units.” “Good idea,” Amal said. “I will do it now.”
Teacher Yasmin walked by. “These numbers are correct. But remember, maths is not only about numbers. It is about facts and smart thinking.”
“Shall we check our pattern again?” Amal asked. “Yes,” said Sami. “Let's see if our division is equal.”
Later that day, each team shared their answers. Amal stood up and said: “We used multiplication and addition. We counted by fives. We measured distance in meters and height in centimeters. Our total number of shells is 375.”
Teacher Yasmin smiled. “You all did well. The answer is … 375 shells. Amal's team found the right pattern.” The class clapped.
Sami whispered, “Teamwork is good because we learn more together.”
The teacher gave a final task. “Write a report using these words: number, measure, pattern, distance. Add one new maths fact.” Sami wrote: “We measured the distance from the bowl. We found a pattern in the numbers. One fact I learned: 25 × 10 = 250.”
As they packed up, Amal said, “I've got a new idea for next week. Let's count the school chairs.” Sami laughed. “Only if we stop before a million!”

comprehension.question: Which maths word does Amal use to add the prices at the market?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She uses addition to add the prices at the market.
comprehension.explanation: The second sentence names addition as the operation Amal uses for prices.

comprehension.question: What do Amal and her mother use to share the dates with four cousins?
comprehension.correctAnswer: They use division to share the dates between the four cousins.
comprehension.explanation: The text says that sharing the dates with four cousins is division.

comprehension.question: Name two things that Amal measures or checks on the way home.
comprehension.correctAnswer: She measures the flour bag and counts her steps for the distance.
comprehension.explanation: The text names the size of the flour bag, its weight and the distance home.

comprehension.question: Which numbers does the pattern poem count, in the order they appear?
comprehension.correctAnswer: One, two, three, four, five, six.
comprehension.explanation: Each line of the poem opens with the next pair of counting numbers.

comprehension.question: What repeats in every line of the pattern poem to make the pattern?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Two counting numbers begin the line and a rhyming word ends it.
comprehension.explanation: Shoe rhymes with two, more with four and sticks with six, so the rhyme repeats.

comprehension.question: In The Measuring Challenge, how many things must the class measure?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The class must measure three things.
comprehension.explanation: The speaker says, “Today we will measure three things,” before listing them.

comprehension.question: What is the first thing the class must measure in The Measuring Challenge?
comprehension.correctAnswer: They must measure the height of their desk first.
comprehension.explanation: The word First marks the opening instruction: measure the height of your desk.

comprehension.question: Which number in Numbers Big and Small is the biggest of them all?
comprehension.correctAnswer: One million is the biggest number in that list.
comprehension.explanation: The list climbs from ten to one million, and one million is spoken last.

comprehension.question: Which number comes just before one million in Numbers Big and Small?
comprehension.correctAnswer: One hundred thousand comes just before one million.
comprehension.explanation: The order is ten thousand, one hundred thousand, then one million.

comprehension.question: What challenge did Teacher Yasmin write on the board for the class?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She asked whether they could find the right pattern to solve the number puzzle.
comprehension.explanation: The board is quoted in the second paragraph, and the class claps after reading it.

comprehension.question: How did Sami work out that the ten bowls held 250 shells?
comprehension.correctAnswer: He multiplied 25 shells by 10 bowls to reach 250 shells.
comprehension.explanation: Amal asks “25 times 10 is …?” and Sami answers “Multiplication!” then “That's 250.”

comprehension.question: Why could Amal not measure the tiny shell, and what did she do next?
comprehension.correctAnswer: It was too tiny to measure, so she used the other ruler.
comprehension.explanation: Sami suggests the other ruler because it has better units, and Amal agrees to try.

grammar.title: Present Simple and Imperatives
grammar.explanation: The Present Simple tells about things that happen often or facts that are always true, like “The sun rises in the east.” An imperative is a command or instruction, like “Measure the height.” Both help us speak and write clearly, especially in maths.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Present Simple: subject + verb (add s for he, she, it). “I count the shells.” / “She counts the shells.”
Imperative: just the verb, with no subject. “Count the numbers.” / “Measure the table.”
Negative imperative: use Don't. “Don't run in the hall.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “He measure the desk.” ✗
Fix: add s for he/she/it: “He measures the desk.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: For an imperative, just say the verb — you are the boss giving one clear order. For he, she, it in Present Simple, don't forget the s!
grammar.practice: ______ (Count) the numbers on the board. (imperative) | She ______ (measure) the rope every morning. (present simple) | ______ (Measure) the height of your desk. (imperative) | We ______ (add) the prices at the market. (present simple)

grammar.title: ‘Have got’ for Ownership
grammar.explanation: We use have got to talk about things we own or have. It helps us tell people about our belongings, like “I've got a ruler.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
I / you / we / they have got (’ve got). “We have got ten bowls.”
He / she / it has got (’s got). “She has got a new ruler.”
Negative: haven't got / hasn't got. “They haven't got any shells.”
Question: Have you got …? “Have you got a meter stick?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “He have got a bicycle.” ✗
Fix: use has got for he/she/it: “He has got a bicycle.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of ‘have got’ like a treasure chest — you open it to show what you own. He, she, it → has got.
grammar.practice: I ______ (have got / haven't got) a new maths book. I love it! | She ______ (has got / hasn't got) a ruler, so she cannot draw a straight line. | We ______ (have got / haven't got) a lot of homework tonight. | ______ you ______ (Have / got) a meter stick?

grammar.title: Can / Can't for Ability
grammar.explanation: We use can to say what we are able to do, and can't for what we are not able to do. It helps us talk about our skills, like “I can measure the distance.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
subject + can + base verb. “You can measure the distance.”
subject + can't + base verb. “You can't subtract letters.”
Question: Can + subject + base verb? “Can you count to a million?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I can swimming.” / “I can measuring.” ✗
Fix: use the base verb after can: “I can swim.” / “I can measure it.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of “can” as a superhero cape. “I can” = cape on, you have the power. “I can't” = cape off.
grammar.practice: I ______ (can / can't) count to one hundred. It is easy! | She ______ (can / can't) reach the top shelf; she is too short. | ______ (Can / Can't) you measure this shell for me, please? | We ______ (can / can't) subtract letters — only numbers!

grammar.title: Demonstratives (This / That)
grammar.explanation: This and that point to things. We use this for something near us and that for something farther away. This makes it clear which thing we mean.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
This (near, one thing). “This is a straight line.”
That (far, one thing). “That pattern is complex.”
These (near, many) / those (far, many). “These are my rulers.” / “Those are your shells.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “That is my pencil,” when the pencil is in your hand. ✗
Fix: it is near you, so use this: “This is my pencil.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Say it like a rhyme: “This is near, that is clear (over there)!”
grammar.practice: ______ (This / That) shell in my hand is very small. | ______ (This / That) tree over there is very tall. | Look at ______ (this / that) straight line I drew here! | Do you see ______ (this / those) mountains far in the distance?

grammar.title: Future with ‘Will’
grammar.explanation: We use will to talk about the future — promises, plans and predictions. It tells what has not happened yet, like “I will measure the table tomorrow.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
subject + will + base verb. “I will multiply the numbers.”
Negative: will not (won't). “I won't forget the answer.”
Question: Will + subject + base verb? “Will you measure it tomorrow?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I will multiplied the numbers.” ✗
Fix: use the base verb after will: “I will multiply the numbers.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of “will” as a magic word for time travel to tomorrow. “I will” is your ticket to the future!
grammar.practice: I ______ (read) a new maths book next week. | They ______ (measure) the classroom tomorrow. | She ______ (count) the shells later. | We ______ (not / forget) to check the pattern.

grammar.title: Short Future Responses + Question Forms + Cause and Effect with ‘Because’
grammar.explanation: Short future answers agree with I will and refuse with I won't. Questions hunt for information, so we swap the subject and the helping verb. Because joins what happened to the reason why it happened.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Short answers: “I will!” to agree; “I won't.” to refuse. Other subjects work too: “She will.” / “They won't.”
Yes/No questions start with a helping verb: “Can you count?” / “Is the line straight?”
Wh- questions start with a question word: “What is the distance?” / “How many shells are there?”
Suggestions use Shall: “Shall I measure the size?”
Cause and effect: effect + because + cause. “I checked again because the answer looked wrong.”
You can put the cause first: “Because it was raining, we stayed inside.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I not will.” / “You can play?” / “I added a number.” (Why?) ✗
Fix: “I won't.” / “Can you play?” / “I added a number because the pattern was not complete.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Won't is will not squeezed together. Flip the subject and the helping verb to ask. Because is the glue between what happened and why.
grammar.practice: Answer with “I will” or “I won't”: A: “Will you help me measure the desk?” B: “______, I'd love to!” | A: “Will you count all the way to a million?” B: “______, that would take too long!” | A: “Will you check the pattern again?” B: “______, good idea.” | ______ is the distance from the door to the window? (asks about a thing) | ______ you measure this shell for me? (Yes/No, about ability) | ______ many bowls are on the table? (asks about number) | ______ we check our pattern again? (a suggestion) | Finish each sentence with a reason using because: We worked as a team ______. | I used multiplication ______. | Amal could not measure the shell ______.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Measure and Tell
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose something in your home and measure it with your helper. Then say:
“This is a / an ______. Its height is about ______.”
“Its weight is about ______.”
“The distance from it to the door is about ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you use a measuring word (meter, centimeter, kilo)? Did you speak clearly?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Listen to my measuring recording with my helper present and tell me whether every number had the right unit.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Maths in the Market
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Imagine you are shopping. Practise saying:
“I will buy three bags. (using will)”
“I've got ten shillings. (using have got)”
“Can you measure the flour for me, please? (using can)”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Play the market seller with my helper nearby, and correct me if I mix up will, have got and can.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — Guess My Pattern
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person says the start of a pattern: “Two, four, six, ______?”
The other person guesses the next number and says the rule: “Eight! You are adding two.”
Try shape patterns too: “circle, square, circle, square, ______?” Then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Give me three number patterns and three shape patterns while my helper listens, then check the rule I say.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Big Number Read-Aloud
speaking.origin: Curriculum-reviewed expansion of the source counting task
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Read this list aloud to your helper: ten, one hundred, one thousand, ten thousand, one hundred thousand, one million. Then say one true maths fact about the largest number you read. Record the list twice and keep the clearer recording.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Hear me read the number list with my helper in the room and mark any number I said unclearly.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Explain It with Because
speaking.origin: Curriculum-reviewed expansion of the source story task
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain to your helper how Amal's team reached 375 shells. Give one reason with because, such as “We multiplied because it is faster than adding.” Then answer two follow-up questions about your reason.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me two why questions about the shell challenge while my helper listens, and check that I answer with because.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — One-Minute Measuring Talk
speaking.origin: Curriculum-reviewed expansion of the source measuring task
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a one-minute talk about measuring one room at home. Open with what you measured, give three numbers with their units, and close with the thing that surprised you most.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Time my one-minute talk with my helper and tell me whether my opening, three numbers and closing were all clear.

writing.title: Writing 1 — How-To Instructions
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write four steps to measure the height of your table. Use imperatives (start each step with a verb):
Step 1: Put the meter stick ______.
Step 2: ______.
Step 3: ______.
Step 4: Write down the ______.
writing.modelText: Step 1: Put the meter stick flat on the floor beside the table. Step 2: Hold it straight against the table leg. Step 3: Read the number level with the table top. Step 4: Write down the height in centimeters.
writing.successCriteria: Every step begins with a verb; every step names one measuring action; every step ends with a full stop.
writing.support: Say each step out loud while you act it out, then write the words you said.
writing.extension: Add a fifth step that explains how to check the height a second time.

writing.title: Writing 2 — My Maths Report
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write three sentences using these words: number, measure, pattern, distance. Add one maths fact you know. For example:
“I measured the distance from my bed to the door. I found a pattern in the tiles. One fact I know: 5 × 5 = 25.”
writing.modelText: I measured the distance from my door to my desk. The number of steps was nine. I found a pattern of light and dark tiles. One fact I know: 6 × 6 = 36.
writing.successCriteria: All four unit words appear; the fact is true and can be checked; each sentence starts with a capital letter.
writing.support: Tick each of the four words on your Word Wall as you use it.
writing.extension: Add one sentence that explains why your fact is useful in maths.

writing.title: Writing 3 — A Pattern Paragraph
writing.origin: Curriculum-reviewed expansion of the source pattern work
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write six sentences about one pattern you can see at home or at school. Start with a topic sentence, name the colours, shapes or numbers that repeat, and join two ideas with because.
writing.modelText: Our kitchen floor has a clear pattern. White squares and grey squares take turns. The pattern is easy to follow because the colours never change order. I counted twelve white squares. Then I counted twelve grey squares. The two numbers are equal.
writing.successCriteria: One topic sentence; the repeating part is named; one sentence uses because; six sentences in total.
writing.support: Draw the pattern first, then label what repeats before you write.
writing.extension: Predict the next three parts of your pattern and explain the rule.

writing.title: Writing 4 — Measuring Fact File
writing.origin: Curriculum-reviewed expansion of the source measuring work
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a heading and five true facts about measuring. Use meter, centimeter, gram and kilo at least once each, and number your facts from one to five.
writing.modelText: Measuring Facts. 1. One meter is one hundred centimeters. 2. We measure short things in centimeters. 3. One kilo is one thousand grams. 4. We weigh flour in grams and kilos. 5. A meter stick helps us measure a door.
writing.successCriteria: The heading names the topic; all four units appear; every fact is true; the facts are numbered one to five.
writing.support: Copy one true fact from the vocabulary tables before you write your own.
writing.extension: Add a sixth fact that compares two units, such as grams and kilos.

writing.title: Writing 5 — Reflect and Improve
writing.promptAndInstructions: Reread your maths report. Rewrite three sentences so a reader understands them faster, add two number details, and explain one change you made.
writing.modelText: I changed “the box was big” to “the box was 40 centimeters wide”. The number tells the reader exactly how big it was. I also added the weight in grams.
writing.successCriteria: Three sentences are rewritten; two new numbers appear; one change is explained clearly.
writing.support: Underline any sentence with no number in it, and start there.
writing.extension: Ask your helper which sentence is now clearest, and write down why.

writing.title: Writing 6 — Report the Shell Challenge
writing.origin: Curriculum-reviewed expansion of the source story task
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a short report of the shell challenge for your class notice board. Use number, measure, pattern and distance, and finish with the total the teams found.
writing.modelText: Our class solved the shell challenge. We used a ruler to measure every shell. The distance from the bowl was two meters. We counted by fives and found a pattern. The total number of shells was 375.
writing.successCriteria: All four unit words appear; the report ends with the total 375; past tense is used all the way through.
writing.support: List the numbers from the story first, then build one sentence around each.
writing.extension: Add a closing sentence that gives your team one target for next time.

activities.title: Activities 1 — Match the Maths Word
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each word to the right meaning. Write the letter.
1. addition (a) taking one number away from another
2. subtraction (b) putting numbers together to get a total
3. multiplication (c) splitting into equal parts
4. division (d) adding the same number many times
activities.answerSummary: 1 b, 2 a, 3 d, 4 c. Each operation must be matched to its own meaning.

activities.title: Activities 2 — True or False (Number Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
1. A million is one thousand thousands.
2. A straight line is bent and curved.
3. We use a meter to measure length.
4. In subtraction, 6 minus 2 equals 5.
activities.answerSummary: 1 True, 2 False, 3 True, 4 False. Both false items need the correction written out.

activities.title: Activities 3 — Fill in the Blanks
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Choose the best word: height, weight, distance, pattern.
1. The ______ of the tree is very tall.
2. The ______ of the watermelon is 3 kilos.
3. The ______ from school to my house is short.
4. The red-blue-red-blue design is a colour ______.
activities.answerSummary: 1 height, 2 weight, 3 distance, 4 pattern. Each measuring word fits only one sentence.

activities.title: Activities 4 — Count and Multiply
activities.instructionsAndItems:
There are 25 shells in one bowl. There are 10 bowls.
1. How many shells are there in total? (Use multiplication: 25 × 10)
2. If you take away 5 shells, how many are left? (Use subtraction.)
activities.answerSummary: 250 shells, then 245 shells. Working may be shown as repeated addition.

activities.title: Activities 5 — Sort the Maths Words
activities.origin: Curriculum-reviewed expansion of the Word Wall
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Sort these twelve words into three groups: number words, operation words and measuring words.
number, million, fact, pattern, straight, addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, measure, height, distance
Write one sentence that explains the rule for each group.
activities.answerSummary: Three groups of words, each with one sentence naming what the group has in common.

activities.title: Activities 6 — Shell Story Number Hunt
activities.origin: Curriculum-reviewed expansion of the source story task
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Find six numbers in The Mystery of the Million Shells. Copy each number, then write what it counts or measures in the story.
Start with the numbers written in the teams' books.
activities.answerSummary: Six numbers copied from the story, each labelled with what it counts or measures.

assignments.title: Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement learning portfolio
assignments.instructions:
Submit Writing 1 — How-To Instructions and a recording of Speaking 1 — Measure and Tell. Improve both pieces using the Grade 3 rubrics.
Marking guidance for writing (16 marks): each of the four steps must open with a verb, name one measuring action and end with a full stop; award 4 marks per step.
Marking guidance for speaking (16 marks): the recording must give one height, one weight and one distance, each with its unit, in clear complete sentences; award 4 marks per measurement and 4 marks for clarity.
Return one specific improvement target for the writing and one for the recording.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does the word 'million' mean in this unit?
quizzes.options: A very big number written 1,000,000, or one thousand thousands. | A unit of length equal to one hundred centimeters. | Going the same way without bending or curving, like a ruler edge. | Splitting a number into equal parts so everyone gets the same.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A very big number written 1,000,000, or one thousand thousands.
quizzes.explanation: One million counts things, so it is a number, not a length.

quizzes.question: What does the word 'measure' mean in this unit?
quizzes.options: A design or sequence of things that repeats in the same order. | How far it is from one place to another place. | To find out the size, length or amount of something. | Taking one number away from another to see what is left.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To find out the size, length or amount of something.
quizzes.explanation: Measure is the action; the others name a pattern, a distance and subtraction.

quizzes.question: What does the word 'pattern' mean in this unit?
quizzes.options: How heavy something is when you put it on the scales. | A design or sequence of things that repeats in the same order. | Something that is true and can be checked or proven. | Adding the same number over and over again to find a total quickly.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A design or sequence of things that repeats in the same order.
quizzes.explanation: A pattern repeats in order, like red, blue, red, blue on the classroom tiles.

quizzes.question: In The Mystery of the Million Shells, how many shells did the teams count in total?
quizzes.explanation: Amal reports 375, and Teacher Yasmin confirms the total is 375 shells.

quizzes.question: Which sentence gives a clear instruction, using an imperative?
quizzes.options: Measure the height of your desk. | She measures the rope every morning. | The desk is one meter high. | Will you measure it tomorrow?
quizzes.correctAnswer: Measure the height of your desk.
quizzes.explanation: An imperative starts with the verb and gives the order straight away.

quizzes.question: Which sentence uses 'have got' correctly for one person?
quizzes.options: They has got a meter stick. | We has got ten bowls. | She has got a new ruler. | He have got a bicycle.
quizzes.correctAnswer: She has got a new ruler.
quizzes.explanation: He, she and it take has got, but we and they take have got.

quizzes.question: Which sentence explains a reason with 'because'?
quizzes.options: I used multiplication today. | I used multiplication because it is faster than adding. | Multiplication and addition are both useful maths operations. | Use multiplication now.
quizzes.correctAnswer: I used multiplication because it is faster than adding.
quizzes.explanation: Because joins the effect to its cause, so it tells us why.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from A Pattern Poem: “Five, six, shapes and sticks, _____ and round, patterns found!”
quizzes.explanation: The poem sets straight lines against round ones, so Straight completes the rhyme.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from Maths Is Everywhere: “When Amal helps her mother at the market, she uses _____ to add the prices.”
quizzes.explanation: Adding prices together is addition, which is why the text names that operation.

quizzes.question: In The Measuring Challenge, which tool may the class use?
quizzes.options: A pair of scissors | A colour pencil | A meter stick | A shopping basket
quizzes.explanation: The speaker names a meter stick before telling the class to begin.

liveSessions.title: Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement: Launch and big numbers
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read the unit overview and learn the five words in Group 1.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min count from ten to one million together; 10 min teacher models reading 1,000,000 aloud; 15 min partner number-card sorting; 10 min pattern chant from the poem; 5 min set the Word Wall target
liveSessions.afterSession: Record yourself saying six big numbers and tick them on your Word Wall.

liveSessions.title: Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement: Reading the operation words
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read Maths Is Everywhere and underline every operation word you meet.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min word-meaning retrieval; 10 min shared reading of Maths Is Everywhere; 15 min matching operations to their meanings; 10 min writing one sum for each operation; 5 min exit question on division
liveSessions.afterSession: Finish Activities 1 and bring your matched letters to the next session.

liveSessions.title: Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement: Imperatives, have got and can
liveSessions.beforeSession: Work through Grammar lessons 1 to 3 and try Practice 1.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min quick-fire commands around the room; 10 min teacher models the s on he/she/it; 15 min have got and can't card game; 10 min writing four instruction steps; 5 min feedback on missing verbs
liveSessions.afterSession: Rewrite any instruction step that did not start with a verb.

liveSessions.title: Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement: The measuring workshop
liveSessions.beforeSession: Listen to The Measuring Challenge and collect a ruler and a meter stick.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min unit relay naming centimeters, meters, grams and kilos; 10 min teacher measures a desk aloud; 15 min pairs measure height, weight and distance; 10 min recording the results in a table; 5 min comparing answers
liveSessions.afterSession: Complete Speaking 1 and save your clearest recording.

liveSessions.title: Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement: Patterns, will and because
liveSessions.beforeSession: Play Guess My Pattern at home and prepare one pattern to share.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min pattern warm-up with claps; 10 min teacher models will for predictions; 15 min pattern-rule explanations using because; 10 min drafting the pattern paragraph; 5 min two stars and one target
liveSessions.afterSession: Redraft one sentence of Writing 3 so it gives a reason with because.

liveSessions.title: Numbers, Shapes, and Measurement: Shell challenge showcase
liveSessions.beforeSession: Reread The Mystery of the Million Shells and finish Writing 6.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min story retrieval quiz; 10 min groups retell the shell challenge; 15 min reading reports aloud to the class; 10 min peer feedback on numbers and units; 5 min setting the Unit 9 reading habit
liveSessions.afterSession: Complete the unit quiz and the self-assessment, then list two words to revise.

teacherNotes.note: Model every measuring word aloud with a real ruler before independent work, and check that learners say the unit as well as the number. Accept spoken, drawn or written answers so that learners who are still building writing stamina can show what they know. An adult stays present for every AI-tutor and recording task; no child is left alone with the device. Give feedback on complete sentences, on text evidence from the shell story, and on precise use of height, weight and distance.

Reading and listening prompts:
“Can you read the pattern poem with me and clap the beat?”
“Can you play a listening game where you say a number and I write it?”
“Can you say a measuring instruction and let me act it out?”
Speaking prompts:
“Can you help me practise saying big numbers?”
“Can you play ‘Guess My Pattern’ with me?”
“Can you check if I used the measuring words correctly?”
Writing prompts:
“Can you check if my how-to steps are in the right order?”
“Can you check my maths report and my spelling?”
Number-word prompts:
“Can you quiz me on saying big numbers?”
“Can you give me a pattern to finish?”
“Is this sentence a fact or not: ______?”
Operation prompts:
“Can you give me three addition and three subtraction questions?”
“What is the difference between multiplication and addition?”
Measuring prompts:
“Can you help me use meters and centimeters correctly?”
“What is the difference between height and distance?”
Maths verb prompts:
“Can you quiz me on the maths verbs?”
“Can you help me count by 5s and by 10s?”
Grammar prompts:
“Can you check my grammar answers?”
“Why do we say ‘she measures’ but ‘I measure’?”
“Can you give me five more sentences to practise ‘have got’?”
Every prompt above is used with an adult sitting alongside the learner.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: 1 b, 2 a, 3 d, 4 c. Addition makes a total, subtraction takes away, multiplication adds equal groups, and division splits into equal parts.
1 True. 2 False — a straight line is not bent or curved. 3 True. 4 False — 6 minus 2 equals 4. Both corrections must be written out.
1 height, 2 weight, 3 distance, 4 pattern. Height answers how tall, weight how heavy, distance how far, and pattern what repeats.
1: 25 × 10 = 250 shells. 2: 250 − 5 = 245 shells. Accept repeated addition of 25 ten times as working for the first answer.

Number words: number, million, fact, pattern, straight. Operation words: addition, subtraction, multiplication, division. Measuring words: measure, height, distance. Each rule sentence must name what the group shares.
answerKey.origin: Curriculum-reviewed expansion of the Word Wall

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Accept any six of these: 3B, 8 cm, 2 meters, 150 grams, 500, 25 shells, 10 bowls, 250, 300, 400, 375, 25 × 10. Each copied number needs a label saying what it counts or measures.
answerKey.origin: Curriculum-reviewed expansion of the source story task

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: A very big number written 1,000,000, or one thousand thousands. The other options define meter, straight and division.
To find out the size, length or amount of something. Measure is the only action word among the four options.
A design or sequence of things that repeats in the same order. Repetition is the part that makes it a pattern.
375 shells. 250 is only the ten bowls, and 500 is the clue's lower limit, not the total.
Measure the height of your desk. The others are a present simple statement, a fact and a future question.
She has got a new ruler. The other three swap has got and have got the wrong way round.
I used multiplication because it is faster than adding. Only this option gives the reason as well as the action.
Straight. The poem contrasts straight with round; the three operation words do not fit the rhyme.
addition. The clue is the phrase to add the prices, which only addition matches.
A meter stick. It is the only tool named in the listening text.

selfAssessment.statement: I can read and say big numbers up to one million.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use the words addition, subtraction, multiplication and division.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can measure size, height, weight and distance, and say the unit.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can describe a straight line and explain a repeating pattern.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can give a clear instruction that starts with a verb.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use have got, can and can't to talk about myself.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself
